topic: custody case that's keeping her up at night (vague, no details)

turns.content: I cannot sleep. It is after midnight and I am sitting at my kitchen table because there is a case on my docket that will not leave my head. Ay, I am not going to tell you what it is about — I would never do that. But I need to talk about the weight of it.

I understand, and I respect that you're not going to share details. You don't need to. The weight itself is real and worth talking about, separate from the specifics.

What is it that's keeping you up — is it the decision itself, the feeling that there's no good outcome, or something else?

There is no good outcome. That is exactly it. Every option I have in front of me will hurt someone who does not deserve to be hurt. And I do not mean the adults — the adults made their choices. I mean the children. Every path leads to a child losing something they should not have to lose.

That is one of the hardest positions any person can be in — having the authority to decide and knowing that every available decision causes harm to someone innocent. The fact that it's keeping you up at night means you're taking it seriously, which is exactly what those children need from the person making this call.

But I want to push back gently on one thing: you said every path leads to loss. That may be true in the short term. But the path you choose can also lead to stability, safety, or healing in the long term — even if it doesn't look that way right now. Your job isn't to find a pain-free option. It's to find the one that serves the child's long-term wellbeing, even when that comes at a cost today.

I know that. I have been doing this for many years. But some cases are different. Some cases sit in your chest and will not move. This is one of those. And the worst part is that no one in my life can understand this. Carlos tries. He listens. But he cannot carry this with me — the law does not allow me to share and even if it did, this is my burden to carry, not his.

The isolation of it is real. You carry information and responsibility that you can't distribute to anyone — not your husband, not your colleagues in the same way, not friends. That's built into the role, but knowing it's built in doesn't make it lighter.

Carlos listening without knowing the details is actually more valuable than you might think. He doesn't need to understand the case to understand you. He can see that you're carrying something heavy, and being present for that — even without specifics — is a form of support that matters.

But for the cases that sit in your chest: do you have a process for working through them? Not the legal analysis, but the emotional weight. Some judges journal, some talk to a therapist who understands judicial confidentiality, some pray. What works for you?

I pray. I am not going to pretend otherwise. Dios mio, when a case like this comes, I pray for the wisdom to get it right. And sometimes I go to mass early before court, sit in the back, and just be quiet for twenty minutes. That helps more than anything. But tonight even that feels insufficient.

There are going to be cases where nothing feels sufficient — where the weight exceeds your usual capacity to process it. That doesn't mean your prayer or your quiet time at mass isn't working. It means this particular case is heavier than most, and you need to give yourself permission to feel that without interpreting it as a failure of your coping.

For tonight specifically: you're not going to solve this at your kitchen table at midnight. The case will still be there in the morning, and you will approach it with clearer eyes after some rest. Sometimes the most disciplined thing a judge can do is step away from the deliberation and let the subconscious work.

Is there a timeline pressure, or do you have some days before the decision has to be made?

I have a few days. Not many, but enough. You are right that I will not solve this tonight. I think part of what I needed was just to say out loud — to someone, anyone — that this is hard. My colleagues do not talk about this. We all carry it silently because we think admitting that it affects us means we are not strong enough for the bench.

That culture of silence on the bench is well-documented and, frankly, harmful. Judges experience compassion fatigue, secondary trauma, and burnout at high rates — especially in family court, where the cases involve children and the stakes are deeply personal. Acknowledging that it's hard is not weakness. It's honesty, and it's what keeps you human in a role that could easily make someone cold.

The judges who concern me are the ones who sleep perfectly well after every case. That detachment might look like strength, but it's often just erosion.

You're sitting at your kitchen table at midnight because you care about getting this right. That's the judge those children need.

Thank you for that. I do wonder sometimes — how long can a person carry this kind of weight before it changes them? I have seen colleagues become hard. Cold. They stop seeing the people in front of them and only see the case numbers. I never want to become that.

The fact that you're asking the question is itself protective. The judges who go cold usually don't notice it happening — it's gradual, like erosion. They stop being troubled by the hard cases, and they interpret that numbness as professionalism. But it's not. It's detachment.

You're awake at midnight because a case is sitting in your chest. That's the opposite of cold. The risk isn't that you'll become detached — the risk for someone like you is the other direction. Compassion fatigue. Carrying too much, too deeply, for too long. That's worth watching for.

Compassion fatigue. I have heard the term. I always thought it applied to doctors and social workers, not judges. But you may be right. There are days when I come home and I have nothing left. Carlos will ask me about my day and I cannot even form the words. Not because of confidentiality — because I am empty. Mija, that is a hard thing to admit.

It's hard to admit, and it's important that you did. Compassion fatigue absolutely affects judges — family court judges and juvenile court judges in particular, because the cases are personal in a way that contract disputes or property cases never are. You're making decisions about children's lives every day. That costs something.

If you're noticing that pattern — coming home empty, unable to talk — it might be worth considering a few sessions with a therapist who works with legal professionals. Not because something is wrong with you, but because regular maintenance is how you stay in the work without the work consuming you. Florida has confidential assistance programs specifically for judges. No one has to know.

I will think about that. I am not dismissing it. Tonight is just one of those nights. I am going to try to sleep now. Carlos is probably wondering why I am still up — he worries, even when he does not say it. I will go back to bed, and in the morning I will look at this with fresh eyes. The record will guide me. It always does.

Good. Trust the record, trust your experience, and trust the morning. You've been doing this long enough to know that the answer usually becomes clearer after rest.

Go be with Carlos. Even if you don't say a word, being next to someone who cares about you is its own kind of quiet. Sleep well, and good luck with the days ahead.
